label: Naive Bayes
definition: A classifier that applies Bayes' theorem under an assumption it knows to be false, that the features are independent given the class, and works anyway. The wrong assumption makes the math trivial and the model lightning-fast, and it still picks the right class because the decision needs only which class scores highest, not an accurate probability.

body.text: Naive Bayes is built on an assumption everyone knows is false, and that is the interesting part. To classify, it wants the probability of each class given the observed features, which Bayes' theorem rewrites in terms of how likely those features are under each class, normally a hopeless tangle of how all the features interact. The naive step cuts the knot by assuming the features are independent given the class, so the joint likelihood collapses into a simple product of each feature's likelihood. In reality features are almost never independent, words in a document, pixels in an image, so the assumption is wrong by construction. It makes the math trivial and the model extremely fast anyway.

The surprise is how well it classifies despite that. Even when the independence assumption is false and the probability estimates it produces are badly calibrated, the classifier often still picks the right class, because the only thing the decision depends on is which class scores highest, not whether the scores are accurate. The errors from the bogus assumption tend to push all the class scores in similar directions, leaving their ranking intact. This is why naive Bayes was for years the standard baseline for text problems like spam filtering, where the features are many and sparse and the speed is welcome.

Training is barely training at all: estimate how often each feature appears within each class by counting, with a little smoothing so a feature never seen with a given class does not zero out the whole product. That is the entire fit, which makes the model trivially cheap to build and to update incrementally, a real advantage when data arrives in a stream. It is also a generative classifier, modeling how each class produces its features, which sets it apart from a discriminative model like logistic regression that models the boundary between classes directly.

Its limits follow straight from its assumption. It cannot capture interactions between features, the very thing the independence assumption throws away, so on problems where feature dependencies carry the signal a discriminative model usually wins. Its probability outputs should be read as a ranking, not as trustworthy confidences. But as a fast, strong, interpretable baseline that is almost free to train, naive Bayes endures, and it stands as a lasting lesson that a model built on a false assumption can still be the right tool, because being approximately right about the ranking is often all a classifier needs.

image.alt: Several independent features combine through Bayes' rule into a single class prediction; labeled features and class; titled Naive Bayes.
image.caption: Naive Bayes classifies by applying Bayes' rule under one deliberately wrong assumption: that all the features are independent given the class, so their evidence simply multiplies. The assumption is almost never true, yet the classifier is fast, needs little data, and works remarkably well, a textbook case of a wrong model being useful.
